definiteIndefiniteQuestOne.title: Battle for Endings
definiteIndefiniteQuestOne.description: Mixed practice: indefinite and definite articles in sentences.
definiteIndefiniteQuestOne.goal: Solidify the difference in endings using real phrases!
definiteIndefiniteQuestOne.questOne: Enter the form (alt): Dort steht ein ___ Haus.
definiteIndefiniteQuestOne.questOneTip: Indefinite article (unknown object). The article 'ein' is empty, so the adjective takes the -es ending from the article 'das'.
definiteIndefiniteQuestOne.questTwo: Enter the form (alt): Das ___ Haus ist sehr schön.
definiteIndefiniteQuestOne.questTwoTip: Definite article (specifically known object). The article 'das' already showed the gender, so the adjective takes the -e ending from the article.
definiteIndefiniteQuestOne.questThree: Choose the correct option: I see a new TV
definiteIndefiniteQuestOne.questThreeTip: Indefinite article, masculine, Akkusativ. The article 'einen' already has an ending, so the adjective takes the -en ending from the article.
definiteIndefiniteQuestOne.questFour: Choose the correct option: I will buy this new TV
definiteIndefiniteQuestOne.questFourTip: Definite article in Akkusativ. The article 'den' has changed, so the adjective takes the -en ending from the article.
definiteIndefiniteQuestOne.questFive: Assemble: A small cat is sitting in the garden
definiteIndefiniteQuestOne.questFiveTip: Feminine. After the article 'eine', the adjective takes the -e ending from the article.
definiteIndefiniteQuestOne.questSix: Assemble: This small cat is very cute
definiteIndefiniteQuestOne.questSixTip: The definite article 'die' already showed everything, so the adjective takes the -e ending from the article.
definiteIndefiniteQuestOne.questSeven: Enter the form (gut): Er ist ein ___ Freund.
definiteIndefiniteQuestOne.questSevenTip: Indefinite article (unknown object). Masculine (der). The article 'ein' is empty, so the adjective takes the -er ending from the article 'der'.
definiteIndefiniteQuestOne.questEight: Enter the form (gut): Der ___ Freund hat mir geholfen.
definiteIndefiniteQuestOne.questEightTip: Definite article (specifically known). The article 'der' already showed the gender, so the adjective takes the -e ending from the article.
definiteIndefiniteQuestOne.questNine: Choose the correct option: We ate a tasty ice cream
definiteIndefiniteQuestOne.questNineTip: Neuter (das Eis). The article 'ein' is empty, so the adjective takes the -es ending from the article 'das'.
definiteIndefiniteQuestOne.questTen: Choose the correct option: This tasty ice cream was cold
definiteIndefiniteQuestOne.questTenTip: The article 'das' already showed the neuter gender, so the adjective takes the -e ending from the article.
definiteIndefiniteQuestOne.questEleven: Assemble: I will buy a new laptop
definiteIndefiniteQuestOne.questElevenTip: Masculine, Akkusativ. The article 'einen' has an ending, so the adjective takes the -en ending from the article.
definiteIndefiniteQuestOne.questTwelve: Assemble: I met that very old neighbor
definiteIndefiniteQuestOne.questTwelveTip: Akkusativ, masculine. After the article 'den', the adjective takes the -en ending from the article.
definiteIndefiniteQuestOne.questThirteen: Enter the form (neu): Das ist ein ___ Computer.
definiteIndefiniteQuestOne.questThirteenTip: Indefinite article. Masculine (der). The article 'ein' is empty, so the adjective takes the -er ending from the article 'der'.
definiteIndefiniteQuestOne.questFourteen: Enter the form (neu): Der ___ Computer ist sehr schnell.
definiteIndefiniteQuestOne.questFourteenTip: Definite article. The article 'der' already showed the gender, so the adjective takes the -e ending from the article.
definiteIndefiniteQuestOne.questFifteen: Choose the correct option: Mom bought a blue cup
definiteIndefiniteQuestOne.questFifteenTip: Feminine. After the article 'eine', the adjective takes the -e ending from the article.

definiteIndefiniteQuestTwo.title: Article Logic: Finale
definiteIndefiniteQuestTwo.description: Mixed training. Learning to distinguish between when we know the object and when we see it for the first time.
definiteIndefiniteQuestTwo.goal: Solidify the difference in endings once and for all!
definiteIndefiniteQuestTwo.questOne: Enter the form (alt): Dort steht ein ___ Haus.
definiteIndefiniteQuestTwo.questOneTip: The object is mentioned for the first time (unknown). The article 'ein' is empty, so the adjective takes the -es ending from the article 'das'.
definiteIndefiniteQuestTwo.questTwo: Enter the form (alt): Das ___ Haus ist sehr teuer.
definiteIndefiniteQuestTwo.questTwoTip: The object is already familiar (we know it). The definite article 'das' already showed everything, so the adjective takes the -e ending from the article.
definiteIndefiniteQuestTwo.questThree: Choose the correct option: In the room there is a new wardrobe
definiteIndefiniteQuestTwo.questThreeTip: The object is mentioned for the first time (unknown). Wardrobe is masculine (der). The article 'ein' is empty, so the adjective takes the -er ending from the article 'der'.
definiteIndefiniteQuestTwo.questFour: Choose the correct option: This new wardrobe is very big
definiteIndefiniteQuestTwo.questFourTip: The object is already familiar (known). The article 'der' already showed the gender, so the adjective takes the -e ending from the article.
definiteIndefiniteQuestTwo.questFive: Assemble: Mom bought a tasty pizza
definiteIndefiniteQuestTwo.questFiveTip: Object mentioned for the first time. Feminine: the adjective takes the -e ending from the article 'eine'.
definiteIndefiniteQuestTwo.questSix: Assemble: This tasty pizza is still hot
definiteIndefiniteQuestTwo.questSixTip: Object is already familiar. The article 'die' already showed everything, so the adjective takes the -e ending from the article.
definiteIndefiniteQuestTwo.questSeven: Enter the form (groß): Dort ist ein ___ Garten.
definiteIndefiniteQuestTwo.questSevenTip: Object mentioned for the first time. Garden is masculine (der). The article 'ein' is empty, so the adjective takes the -er ending from the article 'der'.
definiteIndefiniteQuestTwo.questEight: Enter the form (groß): Der ___ Garten ist sehr schön.
definiteIndefiniteQuestTwo.questEightTip: Object is already familiar. The article 'der' already showed everything, so the adjective takes the -e ending from the article.
definiteIndefiniteQuestTwo.questNine: Choose the correct option: I will buy a new laptop
definiteIndefiniteQuestTwo.questNineTip: Object mentioned for the first time. Masculine, Akkusativ. The article 'einen' has a case ending, so the adjective takes the -en ending from the article.
definiteIndefiniteQuestTwo.questTen: Choose the correct option: I will fix this new laptop
definiteIndefiniteQuestTwo.questTenTip: Object is already familiar. In masculine Akkusativ, after any changed article (den), the adjective takes the -en ending from the article.
definiteIndefiniteQuestTwo.questEleven: Enter the form (alt): Ich habe in einem ___ Hotel geschlafen.
definiteIndefiniteQuestTwo.questElevenTip: Object mentioned for the first time but in Dativ. The article 'einem' already has an ending, so the adjective takes the -en ending from the article.
definiteIndefiniteQuestTwo.questTwelve: Enter the form (alt): In dem ___ Hotel war es laut.
definiteIndefiniteQuestTwo.questTwelveTip: Object is already familiar (Dativ). After the definite article 'dem', which already showed the case, the adjective takes the -en ending from the article.
definiteIndefiniteQuestTwo.questThirteen: Assemble: A little child is laughing
definiteIndefiniteQuestTwo.questThirteenTip: Object mentioned for the first time. Child is neuter (das). The article 'ein' is empty, the adjective takes the -es ending from the article 'das'.
definiteIndefiniteQuestTwo.questFourteen: Assemble: This little child is laughing
definiteIndefiniteQuestTwo.questFourteenTip: Object is already familiar. The article 'das' already showed the gender, so the adjective takes the -e ending from the article.
definiteIndefiniteQuestTwo.questFifteen: Choose the correct option: He has a fast car
definiteIndefiniteQuestTwo.questFifteenTip: Object mentioned for the first time. Car is neuter (das). The article 'ein' is empty, the adjective takes the -es ending from the article 'das'.

definiteIndefiniteQuestThree.title: Master of Endings

definiteIndefiniteQuestThree.questFour: Choose the correct option: This big tree is very old
definiteIndefiniteQuestThree.questFourTip: The object is precisely known. The article 'der' has already shown everything, so the adjective takes the -e ending from the article.
definiteIndefiniteQuestThree.questFive: Assemble: She has a new bag
definiteIndefiniteQuestThree.questFiveTip: Object mentioned for the first time. Feminine: the adjective takes the -e ending from the article 'eine'.
definiteIndefiniteQuestThree.questSix: Assemble: This new bag is very expensive
definiteIndefiniteQuestThree.questSixTip: The object is precisely known. The article 'die' has already shown everything, so the adjective takes the -e ending from the article.
definiteIndefiniteQuestThree.questSeven: Enter the form (kalt): Er hat ein ___ Bier bestellt.
definiteIndefiniteQuestThree.questSevenTip: Object mentioned for the first time. Beer is neuter (das). The article 'ein' is empty, so the adjective takes the -es ending from the article 'das'.
definiteIndefiniteQuestThree.questEight: Enter the form (kalt): Das ___ Bier steht auf dem Tisch.
definiteIndefiniteQuestThree.questEightTip: The object is precisely known. The article 'das' has already shown the neuter gender, so the adjective takes the -e ending from the article.
definiteIndefiniteQuestThree.questNine: Choose the correct option: I will buy a new TV
definiteIndefiniteQuestThree.questNineTip: Object mentioned for the first time. Masculine Akkusativ: the article 'einen' has an ending, so the adjective takes the -en ending from the article.
definiteIndefiniteQuestThree.questTen: Choose the correct option: I will turn on this new TV
definiteIndefiniteQuestThree.questTenTip: The object is precisely known. In masculine Akkusativ, after any article with an ending (den), the adjective takes the -en ending from the article.
definiteIndefiniteQuestThree.questEleven: Assemble: We are waiting for an old friend
definiteIndefiniteQuestThree.questElevenTip: Masculine, Akkusativ. The article 'einen' has already shown the case, so the adjective takes the -en ending from the article.
definiteIndefiniteQuestThree.questTwelve: Assemble: We are waiting for this old friend
definiteIndefiniteQuestThree.questTwelveTip: Object is known. After the article 'den' in the masculine gender, the adjective always takes the -en ending from the article.
definiteIndefiniteQuestThree.questThirteen: Enter the form (gut): Das war ein ___ Tag.
definiteIndefiniteQuestThree.questThirteenTip: Object mentioned for the first time. Day is masculine (der). The article 'ein' is empty, so the adjective takes the -er ending from the article 'der'.
definiteIndefiniteQuestThree.questFourteen: Enter the form (gut): Der ___ Tag war gestern.
definiteIndefiniteQuestThree.questFourteenTip: The object is precisely known. The article 'der' has already shown the gender, so the adjective takes the -e ending from the article.
definiteIndefiniteQuestThree.questFifteen: Choose the correct option: He found a comfortable chair
definiteIndefiniteQuestThree.questFifteenTip: Masculine, Akkusativ. The article 'einen' has an ending, so the adjective takes the -en ending from the article.

definiteIndefiniteQuestFour.questFifteenTip: Masculine, Akkusativ. The article 'einen' has already shown the case, so the adjective takes the -en ending from the article.

definiteIndefiniteQuestFive.questTwo: Enter the form (alt): Der ___ Schrank ist sehr schwer.
definiteIndefiniteQuestFive.questTwoTip: The object is already known. The definite article 'der' has already shown the gender itself, so the adjective takes the -e ending from the article.
definiteIndefiniteQuestFive.questThree: Choose the correct option: I see a new dress

definiteIndefiniteQuestFive.questTwelveTip: The object is precisely known. The article 'der' has already shown the gender itself, so the adjective takes the -e ending from the article.

definiteIndefiniteQuestSix.title: Article Logic: Reloaded

definiteIndefiniteQuestSix.goal: Bring the declension to automatism without hints in the word order!

definiteIndefiniteQuestSix.questOneTip: Indefinite article (unknown object). The article 'ein' is empty, so the adjective takes the -er ending from the article 'der'.

definiteIndefiniteQuestSix.questTwoTip: Definite article (precisely known object). The article 'der' already showed everything itself, so the adjective takes the -e ending from the article.

definiteIndefiniteQuestSix.questFourTip: Definite article (precisely known object). The article 'das' has already shown the neuter gender, so the adjective takes the -e ending from the article.

definiteIndefiniteQuestSix.questFiveTip: Indefinite article (unknown object). Dog is masculine (der). The article 'ein' is empty, so the adjective takes the -er ending from the article 'der'.

definiteIndefiniteQuestSix.questSixTip: Definite article (precisely known object). The article 'der' has already shown the gender itself, so the adjective takes the -e ending from the article.

definiteIndefiniteQuestSix.questTwelveTip: Definite article in Akkusativ (known object). After the article 'den', the adjective takes the -en ending from the article.

definiteIndefiniteQuestSix.questFourteenTip: Indefinite article (unknown object). Wind is masculine (der). The article 'ein' is empty, so the adjective takes the -er ending from the article 'der'.

definiteIndefiniteQuestSix.questFifteenTip: Indefinite article (unknown object). Girl is neuter (das). The article 'ein' is empty, so the adjective takes the -es ending from the article 'das'.

secretLandsQuestOne.questSixTip: This is Nominativ (masculine). Man is 'der Mann'. Since the article 'ein' has no ending of its own, the adjective takes the -er ending from the article 'der'.

secretLandsQuestOne.questSevenTip: Lamp is feminine. After the article 'eine', the adjective always takes the -e ending.

secretLandsQuestOne.questTen: Enter the form (groß): Wir werden in einer ___ Stadt wohnen.
secretLandsQuestOne.questTenTip: The article 'einer' has a case ending. after any modified form of the indefinite article, the adjective always gets the -en ending.

secretLandsQuestOne.questTwelveTip: The verb 'haben' requires Akkusativ. Phone is neuter (das). The article 'ein' has no ending, so the adjective takes the -es ending from the article 'das'.

secretLandsQuestOne.questFourteen: Enter the form (stark): Wir haben mit einem ___ Mann gesprochen.
secretLandsQuestOne.questFourteenTip: The article 'einem' is in a modified case form. Rule: after an article with a case ending, the adjective always takes -en.
secretLandsQuestOne.questFifteen: Enter the form (lecker): Sie isst eine ___ Suppe.
secretLandsQuestOne.questFifteenTip: Soup is feminine (die). The adjective takes the -e ending from the article 'eine'.

secretLandsQuestTwo.questOneTip: Wardrobe is masculine. Since the article 'einen' already has an accusative ending, the adjective always takes the -en ending from the article.

edgeImagesQuestOne.title: Start
edgeImagesQuestOne.description: Practicing the correct declension of adjectives after der, die, das.
edgeImagesQuestOne.questOneTip: Akkusative (masculine): after 'den', the adjective ending is -en.
edgeImagesQuestOne.questTwo: Enter the form (rot): 'Die ___ Blume ist schön.'
edgeImagesQuestOne.questTwoTip: Nominative (feminine): after the article 'die', the adjective ending is -e.

edgeImagesQuestOne.questThreeTip: Nominative (neuter): after the article 'das', the ending is -e.
edgeImagesQuestOne.questFour: I am walking with a kind friend.
edgeImagesQuestOne.questFourTip: Dative (masculine): after the article 'dem', the adjective ending is always -en.
edgeImagesQuestOne.questFive: Enter the correct form (bequem): 'Wir kaufen das ___ Sofa.'
edgeImagesQuestOne.questFiveTip: Akkusative (neuter): the article 'das' doesn't change, the adjective ending is -e.

edgeImagesQuestOne.questSixTip: Akkusative (neuter): after 'das', the adjective ending is -e.

edgeImagesQuestOne.questSevenTip: Akkusative (neuter): after 'das', the adjective ending is -e.
edgeImagesQuestOne.questEight: Enter the form (klein): 'Wo sind die ___ Kinder?'
edgeImagesQuestOne.questEightTip: Plural: after the article 'die', the ending is always -en.
edgeImagesQuestOne.questNine: Listen and write what you hear
edgeImagesQuestOne.questNineTip: Dative (feminine): after the changed article 'der', the adjective ending is -en.
edgeImagesQuestOne.questTen: What does the man often drink?
edgeImagesQuestOne.questTenTip: Akkusative (masculine): the article changed to 'den', so we use the -en ending.
edgeImagesQuestOne.questEleven: Where is the long street located?
edgeImagesQuestOne.questElevenTip: Nominative (feminine): after the basic article 'die', the ending is -e.
edgeImagesQuestOne.questTwelve: Enter the form (groß): 'Wir wohnen in dem ___ Haus.'
edgeImagesQuestOne.questTwelveTip: Dative (neuter): after 'dem', the adjective takes the -en ending.

edgeImagesQuestOne.questThirteenTip: Nominative (masculine): after the basic article 'der', the adjective ending is -e.

edgeImagesQuestOne.questFourteenTip: Akkusative (feminine): the article 'die' doesn't change, the ending is -e.

edgeImagesQuestOne.questFifteenTip: Plural: with a definite article, the ending is always -en.

pastValleyQuestOne.title: Basics of Perfekt
pastValleyQuestOne.description: What did you do? Getting to know the past tense: 'I bought', 'We did'.
pastValleyQuestOne.questSix: Assemble: I did it very quickly.
pastValleyQuestOne.questEightTip: Sehen is a strong verb: the 'ge-' prefix is added, and the '-en' ending is kept. You can see the list of strong verbs in the section: Learning -> Verbs -> Verb Table.
pastValleyQuestOne.questNine: Assemble the question: Did you sleep?
pastValleyQuestOne.questNineTip: For strong verbs (schlafen), the 'ge-' prefix is added and the '-en' ending is kept. You can see the list of strong verbs in the section: Learning -> Verbs -> Verb Table.
pastValleyQuestOne.questElevenTip: Wohnen - remove 'en'. Add the prefix 'ge-' and the ending '-t' at the end.
pastValleyQuestOne.questFourteenTip: Arbeiten - remove 'en'. Add the prefix 'ge-' at the beginning and the ending '-t' at the end.

pastValleyQuestTwo.title: Perfekt: In Motion
pastValleyQuestTwo.description: When we move or change state, 'haben' steps aside. The verb 'sein' takes its place!
pastValleyQuestTwo.goal: Learn to use 'sein' in the past tense.
pastValleyQuestTwo.questOne: Assemble: I went to the cinema.
pastValleyQuestTwo.questTwoTip: In movement situations, the verb 'sein' replaces 'haben'. 'Ankommen' is a strong verb: add 'ge' between the separable prefix and the root. The ending remains.
pastValleyQuestTwo.questFour: Assemble: They went to the sea.
pastValleyQuestTwo.questFourTip: In movement situations, the verb 'sein' replaces 'haben'.
pastValleyQuestTwo.questFiveTip: In change-of-state situations, the verb 'sein' replaces 'haben'.
pastValleyQuestTwo.questSix: Assemble: The children grew up quickly.
pastValleyQuestTwo.questNineTip: In movement situations, the verb 'sein' replaces 'haben'.
pastValleyQuestTwo.questEleven: Assemble the phrase: I fell asleep.
pastValleyQuestTwo.questElevenTip: In change-of-state situations, the verb 'sein' replaces 'haben'.
pastValleyQuestTwo.questTwelveTip: 'schwimmen' is a strong verb with a vowel change in the root. 'ge' is added at the beginning. The '-en' ending remains.
pastValleyQuestTwo.questThirteenTip: In movement situations, the verb 'sein' replaces 'haben'.
pastValleyQuestTwo.questFourteenTip: In movement situations, the verb 'sein' replaces 'haben'.
pastValleyQuestTwo.questFifteenTip: In movement situations, the verb 'sein' replaces 'haben'.

pastValleyQuestThree.title: My Day
pastValleyQuestThree.description: From morning coffee to evening TV shows. Practice Perfekt with real-life activities.
pastValleyQuestThree.goal: Learn to describe your day in the past tense.
pastValleyQuestThree.questOneTip: In change-of-state situations, the verb 'sein' replaces 'haben'.
pastValleyQuestThree.questTwoTip: 'trinken' is a strong verb with a vowel change in the root. 'ge' is added at the beginning. The '-en' ending remains.
pastValleyQuestThree.questFourTip: In movement situations, the verb 'sein' replaces 'haben'.
pastValleyQuestThree.questFive: Select the correct answer: I worked a lot.
pastValleyQuestThree.questSix: Assemble the sentence: We had lunch.
pastValleyQuestThree.questSixTip: 'essen' is a strong verb. 'ge' + 'g' + root (essen) is added. The second 'g' is added to separate the two 'e' vowels in the root and prefix.
pastValleyQuestThree.questEight: Select the correct answer: Did you go grocery shopping?
pastValleyQuestThree.questElevenTip: 'sehen' is a strong verb: 'ge-' is added, and the '-en' ending is kept. You can find the list of strong verbs in the section: Learning -> Verbs -> Verb Table.
pastValleyQuestThree.questTwelveTip: In change-of-state situations, 'sein' replaces 'haben'. The prefix 'ge' is added between the separable prefix and the root of the word.
pastValleyQuestThree.questFourteen: Assemble: I studied German all day.
pastValleyQuestThree.questFifteen: Select: I went to sleep at 11 o'clock.
pastValleyQuestThree.questFifteenTip: In change-of-state situations, 'sein' replaces 'haben'. 'gehen' is a strong verb with a root change. See the list of strong verbs in the section: Learning -> Verbs -> Verb Table.

pastValleyQuestFour.title: Inseparable Prefixes
pastValleyQuestFour.description: Prefixes like be-, ver-, er-, and others remove the ge- prefix. Remember: no 'ge-ge'!
pastValleyQuestFour.goal: Learn not to put ge- where it is forbidden.
pastValleyQuestFour.questTwo: Choose the correct answer: Did you understand everything?
pastValleyQuestFour.questTwoTip: Verstehen is a strong verb with a root change. We do not add the 'ge-' prefix because there is an inseparable prefix. The '-en' ending remains.
pastValleyQuestFour.questFiveTip: Erzählen is a weak verb. The 'ge-' prefix is not added because the verb has an inseparable prefix 'er-'. The '-en' ending changes to '-t'.
pastValleyQuestFour.questEightTip: Erklären is a weak verb. The 'ge-' prefix is not added because the verb has an inseparable prefix 'er-'. The '-en' ending changes to '-t'.
pastValleyQuestFour.questNineTip: Verkaufen is a weak verb. The 'ge-' prefix is not added because the verb has an inseparable prefix 'ver-'. The '-en' ending changes to '-t'.
pastValleyQuestFour.questEleven: Assemble the sentence: You promised that.
pastValleyQuestFour.questTwelveTip: bestellen is a weak verb. The 'ge-' prefix is not added because the verb has an inseparable prefix 'be-'. The '-en' ending changes to '-t'.
pastValleyQuestFour.questThirteenTip: Passieren is a weak verb, but it ends in -ieren. For such verbs, the ge- prefix is never used, and the -en ending changes to -t.
pastValleyQuestFour.questFourteenTip: Erkennen is a strong (mixed) verb. The ge- prefix is not added because of the inseparable prefix er-. The root changes (e > a), but the -en ending remains.
pastValleyQuestFour.questFifteen: Select: We were late (missed the time).
pastValleyQuestFour.questFifteenTip: verspäten is a weak verb with an inseparable prefix ver-. Therefore, the ge- prefix is not added. The -en ending changes to -t.

pastValleyQuestFive.title: Restaurant
pastValleyQuestFive.description: Ordering food, paying the bill, and sharing impressions of a delicious dinner in Perfekt.
pastValleyQuestFive.goal: Learn to use Perfekt in the context of food and restaurants.
pastValleyQuestFive.questOneTip: bestellen is a weak verb. The ge- prefix is not added because it has an inseparable prefix be-. The -en ending changes to -t.
pastValleyQuestFive.questTwo: Select: Did you like the food?
pastValleyQuestFive.questTwoTip: schmecken is a weak verb. It has no inseparable prefix, so the ge- prefix is added. The -en ending changes to -t. Used with the auxiliary verb haben.
pastValleyQuestFive.questThreeTip: Tip: trinken is a strong verb. In the Partizip II form, the ge- prefix is added, and the -en ending is kept. A vowel change occurs in the root (i changes to u).
pastValleyQuestFive.questFiveTip: reservieren is a weak verb ending in -ieren. For these verbs, the ge- prefix is never added, and the -en ending changes to -t.
pastValleyQuestFive.questSixTip: nehmen is a strong verb. In the Partizip II form, the ge- prefix is added, and the -en ending is kept. The root changes significantly (neh > nomm).
pastValleyQuestFive.questEight: Choose the correct answer: She cooked soup.
pastValleyQuestFive.questNine: Assemble: We went to a restaurant.
pastValleyQuestFive.questNineTip: gehen is a strong verb. The ge- prefix is added, and the -en ending is kept. The root changes (geh > gang). Used with the auxiliary verb sind.
pastValleyQuestFive.questTenTip: essen is a strong verb. In Partizip II, the ge- prefix is added and the -en ending is kept. A -g- is inserted between the prefix and the root for easier pronunciation.
pastValleyQuestFive.questElevenTip: bringen is a mixed verb. It has no inseparable prefix, so ge- is added. The root changes (bring > brach), but it uses the 'weak' ending -t.
pastValleyQuestFive.questTwelveTip: vergessen is a strong verb. The ge- prefix is not added because it has an inseparable prefix ver-. The root does not change, and the -en ending is kept.
pastValleyQuestFive.questFourteen: Assemble the question: Did you invite friends?
pastValleyQuestFive.questFourteenTip: einladen is a strong verb with a separable prefix ein-. The ge- prefix is placed between the separable prefix and the root. The -en ending is kept.
pastValleyQuestFive.questFifteenTip: finden is a strong verb. The ge- prefix is added, and the -en ending is kept. A vowel change occurs in the root (i changes to u).

pastValleyQuestSix.title: Travel
pastValleyQuestSix.description: Packing bags, flying to the sea, and hiking in the mountains. Everything in the past tense!
pastValleyQuestSix.goal: Reinforce Perfekt in the context of trips and vacations.
pastValleyQuestSix.questThreeTip: Tip: fahren is a strong verb. In Partizip II, the ge- prefix is added, and the -en ending is kept. Used with the auxiliary verb sein as it indicates movement.
pastValleyQuestSix.questFour: Assemble: The plane took off on time.
pastValleyQuestSix.questFourTip: abfliegen is a strong verb with a separable prefix ab-. The ge- prefix is placed between the separable prefix and the root. The -en ending is kept. Used with sein.
pastValleyQuestSix.questFive: Select: The flight lasted three hours.
pastValleyQuestSix.questFiveTip: dauern is a weak verb. It has no inseparable prefix, so ge- is added. The -en ending changes to -t.
pastValleyQuestSix.questSevenTip: ankommen is a strong verb with a separable prefix an-. The ge- prefix is placed between the separable prefix and the root. The -en ending is kept. Used with sein.
pastValleyQuestSix.questEight: Select: Did you swim a lot?
pastValleyQuestSix.questEightTip: schwimmen is a strong verb. The ge- prefix is added, and the -en ending remains. Vowel change in the root (i to o). Used with sein.
pastValleyQuestSix.questEleven: Assemble: I walked in the mountains.
pastValleyQuestSix.questTwelve: Select: She sunbathed on the beach.
pastValleyQuestSix.questTwelveTip: sonnen is a weak verb. It has no inseparable prefix, so ge- is added. The -en ending changes to -t.
pastValleyQuestSix.questFourteen: Assemble: We really liked the vacation.
pastValleyQuestSix.questFifteenTip: erholen is a weak reflexive verb. The ge- prefix is not added because of the inseparable prefix er-. The -en ending changes to -t. Reflexive verbs (sich) always use haben.

pastValleyQuestSeven.title: Office
pastValleyQuestSeven.description: Calling clients, writing reports, and arguing with the boss. Everything in the past tense!
pastValleyQuestSeven.goal: Practice Perfekt in a working and professional environment.
pastValleyQuestSeven.questOneTip: schreiben — strong verb. Prefix ge- is added, and the -en ending is kept. A vowel change occurs in the root (ei changes to ie).
pastValleyQuestSeven.questFour: Assemble: Did you find the document?
pastValleyQuestSeven.questFourTip: finden — strong verb. Prefix ge- is added, and the -en ending is kept. A vowel change occurs in the root (i changes to u).
pastValleyQuestSeven.questSix: Assemble the sentence: He printed the document.
pastValleyQuestSeven.questSixTip: ausdrucken — weak verb with a separable prefix aus-. In Partizip II, ge- is placed between the separable prefix and the root. The -en ending changes to -t.
pastValleyQuestSeven.questEight: Select: He turned off the computer.
pastValleyQuestSeven.questTenTip: erklären — weak verb with an inseparable prefix er-. No ge- is added, and the -en ending changes to -t.
pastValleyQuestSeven.questElevenTip: verstehen — strong verb with an inseparable prefix ver-. ge- is not added. The root changes (steh > stand), but the -en ending is kept.
pastValleyQuestSeven.questTwelve: Select: They talked to each other.
pastValleyQuestSeven.questTwelveTip: sprechen — strong verb. ge- is added, and the -en ending is kept. A vowel change occurs in the root (e to o).
pastValleyQuestSeven.questThirteenTip: vorbereiten — weak verb. Prefix ge- is not added as the verb has an inseparable prefix vor-. The -en ending changes to -t.
pastValleyQuestSeven.questFourteen: Assemble: When did you finish the project?
pastValleyQuestSeven.questFifteenTip: funktionieren — weak verb ending in -ieren. Prefix ge- is not added, and the -en ending changes to -t.

pastValleyQuestEight.title: Studies
pastValleyQuestEight.description: Learning words, reading texts, and taking tests. The whole student journey in the past tense!
pastValleyQuestEight.goal: Reinforce Perfekt with verbs of learning and communication.
pastValleyQuestEight.questTwo: Select: Did you understand the rule?
pastValleyQuestEight.questTwoTip: verstehen — strong verb with an inseparable prefix ver-. ge- is not added. The root changes (steh > stand), and the -en ending is kept.
pastValleyQuestEight.questFour: Assemble the sentence: We spoke a lot of German.
pastValleyQuestEight.questFourTip: sprechen — strong verb. ge- is added, and the -en ending is kept. A vowel change occurs (e to o).
pastValleyQuestEight.questFive: Select: The course started on Monday.
pastValleyQuestEight.questSix: Assemble: He wrote down this word.
pastValleyQuestEight.questSixTip: aufschreiben — strong verb with a separable prefix auf-. ge- is placed between the prefix and the root. The root changes (ei > ie), and -en is kept.
pastValleyQuestEight.questSevenTip: wiederholen — weak verb with an inseparable prefix wieder-. ge- is not added, and the -en ending changes to -t.
pastValleyQuestEight.questEight: Select: Did you forget the grammar?
pastValleyQuestEight.questEightTip: vergessen — strong verb with an inseparable prefix ver-. ge- is not added, and the -en ending is kept.
pastValleyQuestEight.questEleven: Assemble the sentence: You corrected the mistake.
pastValleyQuestEight.questElevenTip: korrigieren — weak verb. ge- is not added because it ends in -ieren. The -en ending changes to -t.
pastValleyQuestEight.questTwelve: Select the correct one: I looked up the word in the dictionary.
pastValleyQuestEight.questTwelveTip: nachschlagen — strong verb with a separable prefix nach-. ge- is placed between the prefix and the root. The -en ending is kept.
pastValleyQuestEight.questThirteenTip: geben — strong verb. ge- is added, and the -en ending is kept. The root does not change.
pastValleyQuestEight.questFourteenTip: übersetzen — weak verb with an inseparable prefix über-. ge- is not added, and the -en ending changes to -t.
pastValleyQuestEight.questFifteenTip: bestehen — strong verb with an inseparable prefix be-. ge- is not added. The root changes (steh > stand), and the -en ending is kept.

pastValleyQuestNine.title: Party
pastValleyQuestNine.description: Celebrating, meeting people, and calling a taxi. The whole evening in Perfekt format!
pastValleyQuestNine.goal: Practice Perfekt in the context of relaxing with friends.
pastValleyQuestNine.questOneTip: feiern — weak verb. ge- is added, and -en changes to -t. The root does not change.
pastValleyQuestNine.questTwo: Select: Did you invite your friends?
pastValleyQuestNine.questThreeTip: mitbringen — mixed verb with a separable prefix mit-. ge- is placed between the prefix and the root. The root changes (bring > brach), and it takes the -t ending.
pastValleyQuestNine.questFive: Select: Did you meet new people?
pastValleyQuestNine.questFiveTip: kennenlernen — weak verb with a separable prefix kennen-. ge- is placed between the prefix and the root. The -en ending changes to -t.
pastValleyQuestNine.questEightTip: rufen — strong verb. ge- is added, and the -en ending is kept.
pastValleyQuestNine.questNineTip: kommen — strong verb. In Partizip II, ge- is added, and the -en ending is kept.
pastValleyQuestNine.questEleven: Assemble: Did you stay until the end?
pastValleyQuestNine.questElevenTip: bleiben — strong verb. ge- is added, and the -en ending is kept. A vowel change occurs (ei to ie). Auxiliary verb is sein.
pastValleyQuestNine.questThirteenTip: treffen — strong verb. ge- is added, and the -en ending is kept. A vowel change occurs (e to o).
pastValleyQuestNine.questFourteen: Assemble: I went home by bus.
pastValleyQuestNine.questFifteenTip: aufhören — weak verb. ge- is placed between the separable prefix and the root. The root doesn't change, so -en changes to -t.

pastValleyQuestTen.title: The City
pastValleyQuestTen.description: Buying tickets, changing trains, and finding the way to the Town Hall. Everything in the past tense!
pastValleyQuestTen.goal: Reinforce Perfekt in the context of moving around the city.
pastValleyQuestTen.questOneTip: verpassen — weak verb with an inseparable prefix ver-. The prefix ge- is not added, and the -en ending changes to -t.
pastValleyQuestTen.questTwo: Select: We got off at the station.
pastValleyQuestTen.questTwoTip: aussteigen — strong verb with a separable prefix aus-. It indicates movement, so we use the auxiliary verb sein. The prefix ge- is placed between the separable prefix and the root. The root changes (ei > ie), and the -en ending is kept.
pastValleyQuestTen.questFourTip: ankommen — strong verb with a separable prefix an-. Use with sein. The prefix ge- is placed between the separable prefix and the root. The -en ending is kept.
pastValleyQuestTen.questFive: Select: Did you find the museum?
pastValleyQuestTen.questFiveTip: finden — strong verb. The prefix ge- is added, and the -en ending is kept. A vowel change occurs in the root (i to u).
pastValleyQuestTen.questSix: Assemble: I transferred to the subway.
pastValleyQuestTen.questSixTip: umsteigen — strong verb with a separable prefix um-. The prefix ge- is placed between the separable prefix and the root. The root changes (ei > ie), and the -en ending is kept.
pastValleyQuestTen.questEight: Select: He went to the center.
pastValleyQuestTen.questNineTip: vergessen — strong verb with an inseparable prefix ver-. The prefix ge- is not added, and the -en ending is kept.
pastValleyQuestTen.questEleven: Assemble: We looked for the way for a long time.
pastValleyQuestTen.questThirteen: Speak: Did you see the cathedral?
pastValleyQuestTen.questThirteenTip: sehen — strong verb. In the Partizip II form, the prefix ge- is added, and the -en ending is kept. The root does not change.
pastValleyQuestTen.questFourteen: Assemble: I got lost in the city.
pastValleyQuestTen.questFourteenTip: verirren — weak verb with an inseparable prefix ver-. The prefix ge- is not added, and the -en ending changes to -t. The root does not change.
pastValleyQuestTen.questFifteen: Select: We stood at the stop.
pastValleyQuestTen.questFifteenTip: stehen — strong verb. The prefix ge- is added, and the -en ending is kept. A vowel change occurs (e to a). Auxiliary verb is haben (as it is a state of rest, not movement).

pastValleyQuestEleven.title: Family
pastValleyQuestEleven.description: Visiting parents, giving gifts, and chatting about life. Everything in the past tense!
pastValleyQuestEleven.goal: Practice Perfekt on the topic of family relationships and meetings.
pastValleyQuestEleven.questOneTip: besuchen — weak verb with an inseparable prefix be-. The prefix ge- is not added, and the -en ending changes to -t. The root does not change.
pastValleyQuestEleven.questTwo: Select: We looked at old photos.
pastValleyQuestEleven.questThree: Write what you hear: I brought flowers.
pastValleyQuestEleven.questThreeTip: mitbringen — mixed verb with a separable prefix mit-. The prefix ge- is placed between the separable prefix and the root. The root changes (bring > brach), and the ending changes to -t.
pastValleyQuestEleven.questFour: Assemble: He gave me a book.
pastValleyQuestEleven.questFourTip: schenken — weak verb. The prefix ge- is added, and the -en ending changes to -t. The root does not change.
pastValleyQuestEleven.questFive: Select: We talked for a long time.
pastValleyQuestEleven.questFiveTip: sprechen — strong verb. The prefix ge- is added, and the -en ending is kept. A vowel change occurs (e to o).
pastValleyQuestEleven.questSix: Assemble: Did you help your brother?
pastValleyQuestEleven.questSixTip: helfen — strong verb. The prefix ge- is added, and the -en ending is kept. A vowel change occurs (e to o).
pastValleyQuestEleven.questSevenTip: erzählen — weak verb with an inseparable prefix er-. The prefix ge- is not added, and the -en ending changes to -t. The root does not change.
pastValleyQuestEleven.questEight: Select: We went for a walk.
pastValleyQuestEleven.questNineTip: bekommen — strong verb with an inseparable prefix be-. The prefix ge- is not added, and the -en ending is kept.
pastValleyQuestEleven.questTenTip: vorbeikommen — strong verb with a separable prefix vorbei-. The prefix ge- is placed between the separable prefix and the root. The -en ending is kept.
pastValleyQuestEleven.questTwelveTip: gefallen — strong verb with an inseparable prefix ge-. Since the root already starts with ge-, a second prefix is not added. The -en ending is kept.
pastValleyQuestEleven.questThirteenTip: bedanken — weak reflexive verb with an inseparable prefix be-. The prefix ge- is not added, and the -en ending changes to -t. The root does not change.
pastValleyQuestEleven.questFourteenTip: treffen — strong verb. In the Partizip II form, the prefix ge- is added, and the -en ending is kept. A vowel change occurs (e to o).
pastValleyQuestEleven.questFifteenTip: bleiben — strong verb. The prefix ge- is added, the root changes (ei > ie), and the -en ending is kept.

pastValleyQuestTwelve.title: Weekend
pastValleyQuestTwelve.description: Walking, reading, doing sports, and enjoying life. No problems, just Perfekt!
pastValleyQuestTwelve.goal: Reinforce Perfekt with positive and everyday verbs.
pastValleyQuestTwelve.questOne: Assemble: I watched a great movie.
pastValleyQuestTwelve.questTwo: Select: We went to the park.
pastValleyQuestTwelve.questTwoTip: gehen — strong verb. It indicates movement, so we use the auxiliary verb sein. The prefix ge- is added, the root changes (eh > ang), and the -en ending is kept.
pastValleyQuestTwelve.questFive: Select: Did you listen to music?
pastValleyQuestTwelve.questSix: Assemble: We cooked a delicious dinner.
pastValleyQuestTwelve.questSevenTip: treffen — strong verb. The prefix ge- is added, and the -en ending is kept. A vowel change occurs (e to o).
pastValleyQuestTwelve.questElevenTip: besuchen — weak verb with an inseparable prefix be-. The prefix ge- is not added, and the -en ending changes to -t. The root does not change.
pastValleyQuestTwelve.questTwelve: Select: Did you paint a picture?
pastValleyQuestTwelve.questFourteenTip: singen — strong verb. The prefix ge- is added, and the -en ending is kept. A vowel change occurs (i to u).

actionValleyQuestOne.title: Me, You, Him
actionValleyQuestOne.description: Practicing Dative with personal pronouns. Who are we helping and who are we giving gifts to?
actionValleyQuestOne.goal: Master pronouns mir, dir, ihm, ihr, uns, euch, ihnen.
actionValleyQuestOne.questOne: Assemble the sentence: I am helping you.
actionValleyQuestOne.questOneTip: The verb 'helfen' requires the Dative case.
actionValleyQuestOne.questThreeTip: The verb 'glauben' (to believe) requires the Dative case.
actionValleyQuestOne.questFourTip: The verb 'danken' (to thank) only works with the Dative case.
actionValleyQuestOne.questSix: Assemble: She gives me a book.
actionValleyQuestOne.questSixTip: The verb 'schenken' (to give a gift) often requires two objects: to whom (Dative) and what (Akkusativ).
actionValleyQuestOne.questEightTip: The verb 'antworten' (to answer) requires the Dative case.
actionValleyQuestOne.questNine: Assemble the sentence: You must help us.
actionValleyQuestOne.questEleven: Assemble: I am buying you a coffee.
actionValleyQuestOne.questElevenTip: The verb 'kaufen' (to buy) works here as: buy (for whom? — Dative) (what? — Akkusativ).
actionValleyQuestOne.questThirteenTip: The verb 'geben' (to give) follows the scheme: to whom (Dative) + what (Akkusativ).
actionValleyQuestOne.questFourteen: Assemble: I want to bring you some tea.
actionValleyQuestOne.questFifteenTip: The modal verb 'sollst' is in the second position, and 'antworten' goes to the very end. Between them is the pronoun in Dative.

actionValleyQuestTwo.title: With Whom and Where?
actionValleyQuestTwo.description: Using Dative prepositions to describe your movements and plans.
actionValleyQuestTwo.goal: Practice prepositions mit, nach, zu, von, aus, bei, seit.
actionValleyQuestTwo.questOne: Assemble: I am going with a friend.
actionValleyQuestTwo.questTwo: Select: I am going to the doctor
actionValleyQuestTwo.questTwoTip: The preposition 'zu' always requires Dative. In spoken language, 'zu + dem' merges into 'zum'.
actionValleyQuestTwo.questFour: Assemble: He lives with his parents.
actionValleyQuestTwo.questFive: Select: After the course, we go home.
actionValleyQuestTwo.questSix: Assemble: I am going to the station.
actionValleyQuestTwo.questSixTip: The preposition 'zu' (to) always requires Dative. The contraction 'zu + dem' becomes 'zum'.
actionValleyQuestTwo.questEightTip: The preposition 'aus' (out of/from) always requires Dative.
actionValleyQuestTwo.questNine: Assemble: I am at my friend's (female) house.
actionValleyQuestTwo.questEleven: Assemble: We are coming from the city.
actionValleyQuestTwo.questFourteen: Assemble: He is going to the library.
actionValleyQuestTwo.questFourteenTip: The preposition 'zu' requires Dative. The contraction 'zu + der' becomes 'zur'.

actionValleyQuestThree.title: For and Through
actionValleyQuestThree.description: Practicing prepositions für, ohne, durch, gegen, um. Only direct object, only Akkusativ.
actionValleyQuestThree.goal: Consolidate the use of Akkusativ after specific prepositions.
actionValleyQuestThree.questThreeTip: The preposition 'durch' (through) always requires Akkusativ. Park is 'der Park' (masculine), so the article changes to 'den'.
actionValleyQuestThree.questSix: Assemble: I am going without a key
actionValleyQuestThree.questEight: Select: Are we walking around the garden?
actionValleyQuestThree.questNine: Assemble: He is driving through the city
actionValleyQuestThree.questTenTip: The preposition 'um' (around) requires Akkusativ. Lake is 'der See' (masculine). Note: Do not confuse it with 'die See' (sea).
actionValleyQuestThree.questEleven: Assemble: I am buying this for my son
actionValleyQuestThree.questTwelve: Select: To swim against the current?
actionValleyQuestThree.questFifteen: Select: We are walking through the forest
actionValleyQuestThree.questFifteenTip: The preposition 'durch' always requires Akkusativ. Forest is 'der Wald' (masculine).

actionValleyQuestFour.title: Where to or Where?
actionValleyQuestFour.description: Learning to distinguish direction (Akkusativ) and location (Dativ) with two-way prepositions: in, an, auf, hinter, neben, and others.
actionValleyQuestFour.goal: Learn to choose the correct case based on the question: Wohin? or Wo?
actionValleyQuestFour.questOne: Select: I put the vase on the table
actionValleyQuestFour.questOneTip: If there is movement or direction (where to? — wohin?), use Akkusativ. Use the corresponding Akkusativ article for the noun.
actionValleyQuestFour.questTwo: Assemble: The vase is standing on the table.
actionValleyQuestFour.questTwoTip: If the question is 'Where?' (Wo?), use Dativ.
actionValleyQuestFour.questThree: Select: We are going to the cinema.
actionValleyQuestFour.questThreeTip: If the question is 'Where to?' (Wohin?), use Akkusativ.
actionValleyQuestFour.questFourTip: If the question is 'Where?' (Wo?), use Dativ. We are already inside, there is no movement, so use the Dativ article.
actionValleyQuestFour.questFive: Assemble: I put the book in the cupboard.
actionValleyQuestFour.questFiveTip: If the question is 'Where to?' (Wohin?), use Akkusativ. Set the article for the noun in Akkusativ.
actionValleyQuestFour.questSix: Select: The book is lying on the shelf.
actionValleyQuestFour.questSixTip: If the question is 'Where?' (Wo?), use Dativ. Set the article for the noun in Dativ.
actionValleyQuestFour.questSevenTip: If the question is 'Where to?' (Wohin?), use Akkusativ. Set the article for the noun in Akkusativ.
actionValleyQuestFour.questEight: Select: The picture is hanging on the wall.
actionValleyQuestFour.questEightTip: If the question is 'Where?' (Wo?), use Dativ. Set the article for the noun in Dativ.
actionValleyQuestFour.questNine: Assemble: He sits down behind me.
actionValleyQuestFour.questNineTip: If the question is 'Where to?' (Wohin?), use Akkusativ. Use the Akkusativ form for the noun or pronoun.
actionValleyQuestFour.questTenTip: If the question is 'Where?' (Wo?), use Dativ. Use the Dativ form (mir) for the pronoun.
actionValleyQuestFour.questEleven: Select: The cat runs under the bed.
actionValleyQuestFour.questElevenTip: If the question is 'Where to?' (Wohin?), use Akkusativ. Set the article for the noun in Akkusativ.
actionValleyQuestFour.questTwelve: Assemble: The cat is sleeping under the bed.
actionValleyQuestFour.questTwelveTip: If the question is 'Where?' (Wo?), use Dativ. Set the article for the noun in Dativ.
actionValleyQuestFour.questThirteen: Assemble: The cat runs under the bed.
actionValleyQuestFour.questThirteenTip: If the question is 'Where to?' (Wohin?), use Akkusativ. Set the article for the noun in Akkusativ.
actionValleyQuestFour.questFourteen: Select: We are driving to the city.
actionValleyQuestFour.questFourteenTip: If the question is 'Where to?' (Wohin?), use Akkusativ. Set the article for the noun in Akkusativ.
actionValleyQuestFour.questFifteen: Select: We live in the city.
actionValleyQuestFour.questFifteenTip: If the question is 'Where?' (Wo?), use Dativ. Set the article for the noun in Dativ.

actionValleyQuestFive.title: Who? To Whom? What?
actionValleyQuestFive.description: Learning to build sentences with a recipient (Dative) and an object (Akkusativ). Rule: Recipient first, then object.
actionValleyQuestFive.goal: Consolidate word order and object cases.
actionValleyQuestFive.questOne: Select: I give flowers to my mother
actionValleyQuestFive.questOneTip: If there is a question 'To whom?' (Wem?), we use Dative. For the noun (or pronoun), we use the Dative article.
actionValleyQuestFive.questTwo: Assemble: He gives the child an apple.
actionValleyQuestFive.questTwoTip: If there is a question 'To whom?' (Wem?), we use Dative. For the recipient, we use the Dative article. For the object 'What?' (Was?), we use Akkusativ.
actionValleyQuestFive.questThreeTip: If there is a question 'To whom?' (Wem?), use Dative. If there is a question 'What?' (Was?), use Akkusativ.
actionValleyQuestFive.questFour: Select: We buy father a car
actionValleyQuestFive.questFourTip: If there is a question 'To whom?' (Wem?), use Dative. If there is a question 'What?' (Was?), use Akkusativ.
actionValleyQuestFive.questFive: Assemble: She writes a letter to a friend.
actionValleyQuestFive.questFiveTip: If there is a question 'To whom?' (Wem?), use Dative. If there is a question 'What?' (Was?), use Akkusativ.
actionValleyQuestFive.questSixTip: If there is a question 'To whom?' (Wem?), use Dative. If there is a question 'What?' (Was?), use Akkusativ.
actionValleyQuestFive.questSevenTip: If there is a question 'To whom?' (Wem?), use Dative. If there is a question 'What?' (Was?), use Akkusativ - die Regel (feminine).
actionValleyQuestFive.questEight: Assemble: We send them a gift.
actionValleyQuestFive.questEightTip: If there is a question 'To whom?' (Wem?), use Dative. If there is a question 'What?' (Was?), use Akkusativ.
actionValleyQuestFive.questNine: Select: The waiter brings the guest soup
actionValleyQuestFive.questNineTip: If there is a question 'To whom?' (Wem?), use Dative. If there is a question 'What?' (Was?), use Akkusativ.
actionValleyQuestFive.questTenTip: If there is a question 'To whom?' (Wem?), use Dative. If there is a question 'What?' (Was?), use Akkusativ - der Saft (masculine).
actionValleyQuestFive.questEleven: Assemble: Father buys the child ice cream.
actionValleyQuestFive.questElevenTip: If there is a question 'To whom?' (Wem?), use Dative. If there is a question 'What?' (Was?), use Akkusativ.
actionValleyQuestFive.questTwelve: Select: I tell the children a story
actionValleyQuestFive.questTwelveTip: If there is a question 'To whom?' (Wem?), use Dative. If there is a question 'What?' (Was?), use Akkusativ.
actionValleyQuestFive.questThirteenTip: If there is a question 'To whom?' (Wem?), use Dative. If there is a question 'What?' (Was?), use Akkusativ. In the neuter gender, the possessive pronoun in Akkusativ does not change its form.
actionValleyQuestFive.questFourteen: Assemble: I am returning the keys to you.
actionValleyQuestFive.questFourteenTip: If there is a question 'To whom?' (Wem?), use Dative. If there is a question 'What?' (Was?), use Akkusativ.
actionValleyQuestFive.questFifteen: Select: We wish you much luck
actionValleyQuestFive.questFifteenTip: If there is a question 'To whom?' (Wem?), use Dative. If there is a question 'What?' (Was?), use Akkusativ. The word 'viel' is used with uncountable nouns.

actionValleyQuestSix.title: Verb Government
actionValleyQuestSix.description: Who do we help, and who do we see? Practicing verbs that dictate the case themselves (Dativ or Akkusativ).
actionValleyQuestSix.goal: Practice the government of verbs danken, helfen, gefallen, etc.
actionValleyQuestSix.questOneTip: Tip: If there is a question 'To whom?' (Wem?), we use Dative. There is no direct object (What?) in this sentence because 'to thank' only works with a recipient.
actionValleyQuestSix.questTwo: Assemble: He often helps his father.
actionValleyQuestSix.questTwoTip: If there is a question 'To whom?' (Wem?), we use Dative.
actionValleyQuestSix.questThreeTip: Problems is plural. In Akkusativ, the plural form for 'your' remains 'deine'.
actionValleyQuestSix.questFour: Select: My wife likes the dress
actionValleyQuestSix.questFive: Assemble: We must visit our grandmother.
actionValleyQuestSix.questFiveTip: If there is a question 'Whom?' (Wen?), we use Akkusativ.
actionValleyQuestSix.questSix: Select: The student answers the professor
actionValleyQuestSix.questEight: Assemble: Can you find my key?
actionValleyQuestSix.questEightTip: Finden requires Akkusativ: masculine changes the ending to -en.
actionValleyQuestSix.questNine: Select: The bicycle belongs to a friend
actionValleyQuestSix.questNineTip: If there is a question 'To whom?' (Wem?), we use Dative. There is no direct object (What?) in this sentence because 'to belong' only works with a recipient.
actionValleyQuestSix.questTenTip: If there is a question 'What?' (Was?), we use Akkusativ - Das Wort. 'jede+s' takes the ending from the article.
actionValleyQuestSix.questEleven: Assemble: I want to congratulate my brother.
actionValleyQuestSix.questTwelve: Select: I don't like the soup
actionValleyQuestSix.questThirteenTip: If there is a question 'What?' (Was?) or 'How long?', we use Akkusativ.
actionValleyQuestSix.questFourteen: Assemble: He listens carefully to the teacher.
actionValleyQuestSix.questFourteenTip: Zuhören + Dative: the separable prefix goes to the end.
actionValleyQuestSix.questFifteen: Select: Do you need my help?

actionValleyQuestSeven.title: Trap Verbs
actionValleyQuestSeven.description: Calling, visiting, and believing. Practicing verbs where the German case doesn't match logic.
actionValleyQuestSeven.goal: Memorize the government of verbs like anrufen, besuchen, fragen, gratulieren, and others.
actionValleyQuestSeven.questOne: Choose the correct answer: I am calling my father.
actionValleyQuestSeven.questTwo: Assemble the sentence: We are visiting our grandmother.
actionValleyQuestSeven.questSevenTip: If the question is 'Whom?' (Wen?), we use Akkusativ. There is no recipient (To whom?) in this sentence, so Dativ is not used.
actionValleyQuestSeven.questEight: Assemble: I am listening to you carefully.
actionValleyQuestSeven.questEightTip: Zuhören (to listen to someone) is Dativ.
actionValleyQuestSeven.questNine: Select: I am inviting a friend.
actionValleyQuestSeven.questTenTip: Verzeihen (to forgive). If the question is 'To whom?' (Wem?), we use Dativ. There is no direct object (What?) in this sentence.
actionValleyQuestSeven.questTwelve: Select: Do you know this person?
actionValleyQuestSeven.questFifteenTip: Danken requires Dativ (dem). The word 'Kollege' adds -n in all cases except Nominativ.

actionValleyQuestEight.title: Verbs and Their Companions
actionValleyQuestEight.description: Waiting, thinking, and dreaming. Learning to use verbs with their mandatory prepositions and the correct case.
actionValleyQuestEight.goal: Memorize combinations like warten auf, denken an, träumen von.
actionValleyQuestEight.questOne: Choose the correct answer: I am waiting for you.
actionValleyQuestEight.questTwo: Assemble: I am thinking of you.
actionValleyQuestEight.questTwoTip: Denken requires the preposition an + Akkusativ.
actionValleyQuestEight.questFour: Select: I dream of a car.
actionValleyQuestEight.questFourTip: Träumen is always with von + Dativ.
actionValleyQuestEight.questFive: Assemble: I am happy about the gift (already received).
actionValleyQuestEight.questFiveTip: Sich freuen über — to be happy about something that already exists (Akkusativ).
actionValleyQuestEight.questSix: Select: I thank you for the help.
actionValleyQuestEight.questSixTip: Danken (to whom - Dat) + für (for what - Akk).
actionValleyQuestEight.questEight: Assemble: I am interested in music.
actionValleyQuestEight.questNine: Select: I am telling about the trip.
actionValleyQuestEight.questNineTip: Erzählen (to tell) usually goes with von + Dativ.
actionValleyQuestEight.questTenTip: Angst haben (to be afraid) requires vor + Dativ.
actionValleyQuestEight.questEleven: Assemble: I am talking with my brother.
actionValleyQuestEight.questTwelve: Select: We laugh at the joke.
actionValleyQuestEight.questTwelveTip: Lachen (to laugh at something) is über + Akkusativ.
actionValleyQuestEight.questThirteenTip: Sich erinnern (to remember) requires an + Akkusativ.
actionValleyQuestEight.questFourteen: Assemble: He asks about the way.
actionValleyQuestEight.questFourteenTip: Fragen (to ask about something) is nach + Dativ.
actionValleyQuestEight.questFifteen: Select: I am participating in the course.
actionValleyQuestEight.questFifteenTip: Teilnehmen (to participate) is always an + Dativ.

actionValleyQuestNine.title: Masters of Replacement
actionValleyQuestNine.description: Continuing to learn how to replace entire phrases with short words. Practicing daran, wonach, wovor, and others.
actionValleyQuestNine.goal: Automate the use of pronominal adverbs in different contexts.
actionValleyQuestNine.questOne: Select: What are you asking about?
actionValleyQuestNine.questOneTip: Fragen nach — requires a question (about what?).
actionValleyQuestNine.questTwo: Assemble: I am angry about it.
actionValleyQuestNine.questTwoTip: Sich ärgern über -> answer: da + r + über (dadrüber/darüber).
actionValleyQuestNine.questThreeTip: Sprechen über -> question structure: Wo + r + über = Worüber (about what?).
actionValleyQuestNine.questFour: Select: I am used to it.
actionValleyQuestNine.questSix: Select: What do you feel like doing?
actionValleyQuestNine.questSevenTip: Zufrieden mit -> answer: Damit (with it/happy with it).
actionValleyQuestNine.questEight: Select: What are you afraid of?
actionValleyQuestNine.questEightTip: Angst haben vor — question: Wo + vor.
actionValleyQuestNine.questNineTip: Bitten um -> answer: Darum (for it/about it).
actionValleyQuestNine.questTen: Select: What are you starting with?
actionValleyQuestNine.questElevenTip: Hoffen (to hope) auf -> answer: Darauf (for it).
actionValleyQuestNine.questTwelve: Assemble: We are working on it.
actionValleyQuestNine.questThirteen: Select: I am celebrating my birthday and inviting you to it.
actionValleyQuestNine.questFifteen: Select: Shall I look after it?

actionValleyQuestTen.title: Grandmaster of Replacement
actionValleyQuestTen.description: Final grind on pronominal adverbs. Only pure German in the options.
actionValleyQuestTen.goal: Practice advanced links like woran, wovon, wozu and their replacements.
actionValleyQuestTen.questOne: Select: What are you relying on?
actionValleyQuestTen.questOneTip: Verlassen auf requires the question Wo+r+auf (Worauf).
actionValleyQuestTen.questThreeTip: Träumen von requires the question: Wovon (of what?) Wo + von.
actionValleyQuestTen.questFiveTip: Zweifeln an requires the answer: Daran.
actionValleyQuestTen.questSix: Select: What are you complaining about?
actionValleyQuestTen.questSixTip: Beschweren über requires the question Worüber (Wo + r + über).
actionValleyQuestTen.questSevenTip: Wissen + von requires the answer: Davon.
actionValleyQuestTen.questEight: Select: What are you participating in?
actionValleyQuestTen.questEightTip: Teilnehmen + an requires the question: Woran.
actionValleyQuestTen.questNine: Assemble: I am ready for it.
actionValleyQuestTen.questTen: Select: What is it made of?
actionValleyQuestTen.questTenTip: Bestehen aus requires: Woraus (Wo + r + aus).
actionValleyQuestTen.questElevenTip: Einigen auf requires the answer: Darauf.
actionValleyQuestTen.questTwelveTip: Es geht um requires the question Worum (Wo + r + um).
actionValleyQuestTen.questThirteen: Select: I am sure of it.
actionValleyQuestTen.questThirteenTip: Überzeugen von requires the answer Davon (da + von).
actionValleyQuestTen.questFourteenTip: Abhängen von requires the answer: Davon.
actionValleyQuestTen.questFifteen: Select: I congratulate you on that.
actionValleyQuestTen.questFifteenTip: Gratulieren zu requires the answer: Dazu.

actionValleyQuestEleven.title: Final Mix
actionValleyQuestEleven.description: Final challenge. All the main traps collected here: from cases and prepositions to item replacements.
actionValleyQuestEleven.goal: Remember everything and prove that the base of cases and verbs is 100% mastered.
actionValleyQuestEleven.questTwo: Assemble: I go with my friend
actionValleyQuestEleven.questThree: Select: We are walking through the park
actionValleyQuestEleven.questFourTip: When answering 'Where?' (Wo?), we use Dative. The preposition 'in' merges with 'dem' to form 'im'.
actionValleyQuestEleven.questFive: Assemble: I go to the cinema.
actionValleyQuestEleven.questFiveTip: Where to? — Akkusativ. In + das = ins.
actionValleyQuestEleven.questSix: Select: I am calling my father.
actionValleyQuestEleven.questSevenTip: N-declension: Junge gets -n in all cases except Nominative.
actionValleyQuestEleven.questEightTip: When the action is on a body part — use Dative (mir).
actionValleyQuestEleven.questNine: Select: I am waiting for the bus.
actionValleyQuestEleven.questTenTip: Question for an object with 'an': it is Woran (Wo + r + an).
actionValleyQuestEleven.questTwelve: Select: The waiter brings the guest soup.
actionValleyQuestEleven.questFifteen: Select: Who are you talking to?
actionValleyQuestEleven.questFifteenTip: For people, use preposition + question (Mit wem?).

pluralLandQuestOne.title: Travel
pluralLandQuestOne.description: Planning a trip using Futur I. Learning to connect time, place, and action.
pluralLandQuestOne.goal: Practice Futur I with holiday planning examples.
pluralLandQuestOne.questOne: Choose the verb for the future tense
pluralLandQuestOne.questThree: Assemble the sentence: I will pack the suitcase.
pluralLandQuestOne.questFour: Assemble the sentence: I will visit my friends.
pluralLandQuestOne.questSix: Assemble the sentence: He will buy a new car.
pluralLandQuestOne.questSeven: Enter the correct form (will): Er ... mit dem Auto fahren
pluralLandQuestOne.questNine: Assemble the question: When will you go home?
pluralLandQuestOne.questEleven: Assemble the sentence: We will visit many museums.
pluralLandQuestOne.questTwelve: Assemble the sentence: I will study next year.
pluralLandQuestOne.questFifteen: Assemble the sentence: It will be a great trip.

pluralLandQuestTwo.title: Goals
pluralLandQuestTwo.description: Practicing Futur I on personal promises and future predictions.
pluralLandQuestTwo.goal: Consolidate the use of 'werden' in different persons and with negation.
pluralLandQuestTwo.questOne: Assemble the sentence: I will never do that again.
pluralLandQuestTwo.questTwo: Select the answer: He will call you tomorrow.
pluralLandQuestTwo.questThree: Assemble the sentence: I will learn more German words.
pluralLandQuestTwo.questFive: Assemble: What will you cook tonight?
pluralLandQuestTwo.questSix: Assemble the question: Will you help me tomorrow?
pluralLandQuestTwo.questNine: Select the correct negation: I will not smoke.
pluralLandQuestTwo.questTen: Assemble: I will never forget this.
pluralLandQuestTwo.questEleven: Select: I will be waiting for you.
pluralLandQuestTwo.questThirteen: Assemble: Next year I will buy a house.
pluralLandQuestTwo.questFourteen: Assemble: Next year I will go to the sea.

pluralLandQuestThree.title: General Plans
pluralLandQuestThree.description: Planning together. Practicing 'wir', 'ihr', 'sie' forms in the future tense.
pluralLandQuestThree.goal: Learn to talk about joint actions in the future.
pluralLandQuestThree.questOne: Select: When will you go to Berlin?
pluralLandQuestThree.questTwo: Assemble: We will buy tickets tomorrow.
pluralLandQuestThree.questFive: Select: They will complete the project.
pluralLandQuestThree.questSix: Select: I will definitely pass this exam.
pluralLandQuestThree.questSeven: Assemble: You will receive an answer soon.
pluralLandQuestThree.questEight: Assemble: The guests will arrive later.
pluralLandQuestThree.questEleven: Assemble: We will not wait long.

pluralLandQuestFour.title: Hosting a Party
pluralLandQuestFour.description: Planning a celebration! Use Futur I to discuss guests, food, and entertainment.
pluralLandQuestFour.goal: Learn to arrange meetings and distribute tasks.
pluralLandQuestFour.questOne: Assemble: We will have a big party on Saturday.
pluralLandQuestFour.questThree: Assemble: The guests will bring gifts.
pluralLandQuestFour.questFour: Assemble: We will bake a delicious cake.
pluralLandQuestFour.questSix: Assemble the question: When will you come?
pluralLandQuestFour.questEight: Assemble: My parents will not be home.
pluralLandQuestFour.questNine: Assemble: Will you drink juice or cola?
pluralLandQuestFour.questEleven: Assemble: We will decorate the room with balloons.
pluralLandQuestFour.questThirteen: Assemble: We will stay until tomorrow.
pluralLandQuestFour.questFifteen: Assemble: We will listen to music together.

pluralLandQuestFive.title: Sports and Victories
pluralLandQuestFive.description: Use Futur I for match predictions, training sessions, and describing sports achievements.
pluralLandQuestFive.goal: Learn to talk about sports plans and future results.
pluralLandQuestFive.questOne: Assemble: We will definitely win this game.
pluralLandQuestFive.questThree: Assemble: The team will play well.
pluralLandQuestFive.questFour: Assemble: They will train every day.
pluralLandQuestFive.questSix: Assemble the question: Will you go to football?
pluralLandQuestFive.questEight: Choose the correct answer: We'll see.
pluralLandQuestFive.questTen: Assemble: The spectators will shout loudly.
pluralLandQuestFive.questEleven: Assemble: You will be tired after the match.
pluralLandQuestFive.questThirteen: Assemble: Who will win the match?

pluralLandQuestSix.title: Weekend Plans
pluralLandQuestSix.description: How will we spend Saturday and Sunday? Practicing Futur I on the topic of relaxation and weekend chores.
pluralLandQuestSix.goal: Learn to talk about your leisure plans.
pluralLandQuestSix.questOne: Assemble: On Saturday, I will sleep late.
pluralLandQuestSix.questThree: Assemble: My brother will go to the cinema.
pluralLandQuestSix.questFive: Assemble: We will go for a walk in the park.
pluralLandQuestSix.questSix: Assemble the question: What will you do on Sunday? (formal)
pluralLandQuestSix.questEight: Assemble: In the evening, we will watch a movie.
pluralLandQuestSix.questTen: Assemble: I will not work this weekend.
pluralLandQuestSix.questThirteen: Assemble: The movie will last two hours.
pluralLandQuestSix.questFifteen: Assemble: It will be a great weekend.

pluralLandQuestSeven.title: Moving
pluralLandQuestSeven.description: Planning a move. Boxes, furniture, and a new apartment. Using Futur I.
pluralLandQuestSeven.goal: Practice moving vocabulary and future tense grammar.
pluralLandQuestSeven.questOne: Assemble: I will move next week.
pluralLandQuestSeven.questTwo: Assemble: We will need many boxes.
pluralLandQuestSeven.questFour: Assemble: Friends will help us carry things.
pluralLandQuestSeven.questFourTip: Note: after 'beim' (bei + dem), the verb turns into a noun and is capitalized: das Tragen.
pluralLandQuestSeven.questFive: Assemble the question: Who will drive the car?
pluralLandQuestSeven.questSix: Assemble: We will paint the walls white.
pluralLandQuestSeven.questEight: Assemble the question: When will you get the key?
pluralLandQuestSeven.questNine: Assemble: The new apartment will be very beautiful.
pluralLandQuestSeven.questEleven: Select: You will have plenty of space.
pluralLandQuestSeven.questTwelve: Assemble: I will unpack all the things tomorrow.
pluralLandQuestSeven.questThirteen: Assemble: We will assemble the furniture.
pluralLandQuestSeven.questFifteen: Assemble: We will hang the lamps.

pluralLandQuestEight.title: Weather Forecast
pluralLandQuestEight.description: Will it rain or shine? Learning to build weather forecasts using Futur I.
pluralLandQuestEight.goal: Learn to describe the weather in the future tense.
pluralLandQuestEight.questFour: Assemble: It will snow in winter.
pluralLandQuestEight.questSix: Assemble: It will be windy tomorrow.
pluralLandQuestEight.questEight: Select: The sky will be blue.
pluralLandQuestEight.questNine: Assemble: We will need an umbrella.
pluralLandQuestEight.questEleven: Assemble: The wind will be strong.
pluralLandQuestEight.questFourteen: Assemble the question: Will the weather improve?
pluralLandQuestEight.questFifteen: Select: The days will become longer again.

pluralLandQuestNine.title: Health
pluralLandQuestNine.description: A visit to the doctor and treatment. Talking about recovery using Futur I.
pluralLandQuestNine.goal: Practice Futur I in a medical context.
pluralLandQuestNine.questOne: I will go to the doctor.
pluralLandQuestNine.questTwo: Select: The doctor will examine me.
pluralLandQuestNine.questFour: Assemble: You will be healthy soon.
pluralLandQuestNine.questFive: Select: We will take the medicine.
pluralLandQuestNine.questNine: Assemble: She will drink a lot of tea.
pluralLandQuestNine.questTwelve: Assemble: We will make an appointment.
pluralLandQuestNine.questFifteen: Select: He will sleep a lot.

pluralLandQuestTen.title: Shopping
pluralLandQuestTen.description: Going shopping. Clothes, groceries, and prices. Using Futur I.
pluralLandQuestTen.goal: Learn to talk about future purchases.
pluralLandQuestTen.questOne: Assemble: I will buy a new dress
pluralLandQuestTen.questTwo: Select: These shoes will cost a lot
pluralLandQuestTen.questFour: Assemble: The shirt will fit you
pluralLandQuestTen.questFive: Select: I will pay by card
pluralLandQuestTen.questSix: Assemble: He will try on the trousers
pluralLandQuestTen.questEight: Select: We will spend a lot of money
pluralLandQuestTen.questNine: Assemble: The shop will close soon.
pluralLandQuestTen.questTwelve: Select: You will find a gift

pluralLandQuestEleven.title: At the Restaurant
pluralLandQuestEleven.description: Going out for dinner! Booking a table, choosing dishes, and paying the bill. Using Futur I.
pluralLandQuestEleven.goal: Learn to make an order and talk about food in the future
pluralLandQuestEleven.questOne: Assemble: We will reserve a table
pluralLandQuestEleven.questTwo: Select: The waiter will bring the menu
pluralLandQuestEleven.questFive: Select the correct answer: The food will be delicious
pluralLandQuestEleven.questSix: Assemble: We will leave a tip
pluralLandQuestEleven.questNine: Assemble: He will pay the bill
pluralLandQuestEleven.questTwelve: Select: We will wait a long time

pluralLandQuestTwelve.title: Cinema
pluralLandQuestTwelve.description: Shall we go to the cinema? Choosing a movie, buying tickets, and popcorn. Using Futur I.
pluralLandQuestTwelve.goal: Learn to discuss plans for cinema and movies
pluralLandQuestTwelve.questOne: Assemble: We will go to the cinema
pluralLandQuestTwelve.questTwo: Select: The movie will be exciting
pluralLandQuestTwelve.questFour: Assemble: The movie will start soon
pluralLandQuestTwelve.questFive: Select: You will laugh a lot
pluralLandQuestTwelve.questSix: Assemble: Who will buy the tickets?
pluralLandQuestTwelve.questEight: Select: You will like the movie
pluralLandQuestTwelve.questNine: Assemble: I will watch a comedy
pluralLandQuestTwelve.questEleven: Assemble: We will sit at the front
pluralLandQuestTwelve.questFourteen: Assemble: The movie will last long
pluralLandQuestTwelve.questFifteen: Select: It will be quiet in the cinema

pluralLandQuestThirteen.title: Animals
pluralLandQuestThirteen.description: Will we get a dog or a cat? Talking about pets and animals in Futur I.
pluralLandQuestThirteen.goal: Learn to talk about animals in the future tense.
pluralLandQuestThirteen.questOne: Assemble: I will buy a dog
pluralLandQuestThirteen.questSix: Assemble: We will feed the animals
pluralLandQuestThirteen.questEight: Select: You will play with the dog
pluralLandQuestThirteen.questNine: Assemble: Who will walk the dog?

pluralLandQuestFourteen.title: Ecology
pluralLandQuestFourteen.description: Saving the planet. Talking about ecology and nature protection using complex Futur I structures.
pluralLandQuestFourteen.goal: Master ecology vocabulary in the future tense.
pluralLandQuestFourteen.questOne: Assemble: We will protect our nature better
pluralLandQuestFourteen.questFour: Assemble: People will use solar energy
pluralLandQuestFourteen.questSix: Assemble: I will always sort the trash
pluralLandQuestFourteen.questNine: Assemble: Electric cars will become very popular
pluralLandQuestFourteen.questEleven: Assemble: Who will take care of the planet?
pluralLandQuestFourteen.questFourteen: Assemble: We will not pollute the sea

pluralLandQuestFifteen.title: Holidays
pluralLandQuestFifteen.description: Christmas and New Year. Talking about holiday plans in Futur I.
pluralLandQuestFifteen.questOne: Assemble: We will celebrate all day long
pluralLandQuestFifteen.questSix: Assemble: The year will begin soon
pluralLandQuestFifteen.questEleven: Assemble: Will you send a card?
pluralLandQuestFifteen.questFourteen: Assemble: We will not forget the holiday.

orderValleyQuestOne.title: Verb at the end!
orderValleyQuestOne.description: Practicing word order in subordinate clauses. The main rule: the verb goes to the very end.
orderValleyQuestOne.goal: Learn to place the verb in the last position after conjunctions like weil, dass, wenn, and others.
orderValleyQuestOne.questOne: Assemble: I study because it is important.
orderValleyQuestOne.questOneTip: In a subordinate clause after the conjunction (weil), the verb always takes the last position.
orderValleyQuestOne.questTwo: Select: He says that he will come.
orderValleyQuestOne.questTwoTip: The conjunction 'dass' sends the conjugated verb to the very end of the phrase.
orderValleyQuestOne.questThreeTip: Wenn (if/when) is a subordinate conjunction; the verb 'habe' stands at the end.
orderValleyQuestOne.questFour: Assemble: We go home when the movie ends.
orderValleyQuestOne.questFive: Select: I don't know if he will come today.
orderValleyQuestOne.questFiveTip: The conjunction 'ob' (if/whether) works the same way: the verb goes to the end.
orderValleyQuestOne.questSix: Assemble: She works although she is ill.
orderValleyQuestOne.questSixTip: Obwohl (although) is a typical conjunction for a Nebensatz. It sends the conjugated verb to the end.
orderValleyQuestOne.questEight: Select: I am tired because I get up early.
orderValleyQuestOne.questEightTip: For verbs with a separable prefix at the end of a subordinate clause, the prefix does NOT separate.
orderValleyQuestOne.questNine: Assemble: I think that you are right.
orderValleyQuestOne.questNineTip: The conjunction 'dass' (that) is a 'subordinating' conjunction. It forces the verb to move to the very end.
orderValleyQuestOne.questTenTip: Question words (wo, wann, wie) in the middle of a sentence also require the verb at the end.
orderValleyQuestOne.questEleven: Select: We know that we must help.
orderValleyQuestOne.questElevenTip: If there is a modal verb, it is the one (müssen) that stands in the last position.
orderValleyQuestOne.questTwelve: Assemble: I will call you as soon as I arrive.
orderValleyQuestOne.questTwelveTip: In the main part, the prefix 'an' separated. Don't forget that in the subordinate part, the prefix in 'ankomme' stays at the end with the verb.
orderValleyQuestOne.questThirteenTip: Damit (so that) is a subordinate conjunction.
orderValleyQuestOne.questFourteen: Select: Since it's already late, I'm going to sleep.
orderValleyQuestOne.questFourteenTip: Da (since/because). If the subordinate clause comes first, the verb in it is still at the end, and the main clause starts immediately with the verb (gehe).
orderValleyQuestOne.questFifteen: Assemble: He asked where you bought this book.
orderValleyQuestOne.questFifteenTip: In the past tense (Perfekt), the auxiliary verb (hast) stands last, after the participle.

orderValleyQuestTwo.title: Mini-phrases
orderValleyQuestTwo.description: Short phrases and sentences to practice subordinate clauses.
orderValleyQuestTwo.goal: Consolidate the verb position in subordinate clauses using simple examples.
orderValleyQuestTwo.questOne: Assemble: I know that you are here.
orderValleyQuestTwo.questOneTip: After the conjunction 'dass', the conjugated verb goes to the end.
orderValleyQuestTwo.questTwo: Select: He eats because he is hungry.
orderValleyQuestTwo.questTwoTip: After 'weil', the conjugated verb always stands at the very end of the sentence.
orderValleyQuestTwo.questThreeTip: The conjunction 'dass' is subordinating. It forces the conjugated verb to move to the very end.
orderValleyQuestTwo.questFour: Assemble: I will ask if he is coming.
orderValleyQuestTwo.questFourTip: The conjunction 'ob' requires the verb in the last position.
orderValleyQuestTwo.questFive: Select: I don't know where my phone is.
orderValleyQuestTwo.questFiveTip: In an indirect question after 'wo', the verb 'ist' must be at the very end.
orderValleyQuestTwo.questSix: Assemble: We stay home if it rains.
orderValleyQuestTwo.questSixTip: After the conjunction 'wenn', the conjugated verb always stands at the end of the sentence.
orderValleyQuestTwo.questSevenTip: After the conjunction 'obwohl' (although), the conjugated verb must be at the very end.
orderValleyQuestTwo.questEight: Select: I see that you are reading.
orderValleyQuestTwo.questNine: Assemble: We wait until he calls.
orderValleyQuestTwo.questNineTip: For the verb 'anrufen', the prefix does not separate because it stands at the end of the subordinate clause.
orderValleyQuestTwo.questTenTip: In a subordinate clause after the conjunction 'weil', the conjugated verb always stands at the very end.
orderValleyQuestTwo.questEleven: Select: I know where you live.
orderValleyQuestTwo.questElevenTip: The question word 'wo' in the middle of a sentence works like a conjunction and puts the verb at the end.
orderValleyQuestTwo.questTwelve: Assemble: I'm glad that you can come.
orderValleyQuestTwo.questTwelveTip: In a pair of two verbs, the conjugated (modal) verb is always placed after the infinitive at the very end.
orderValleyQuestTwo.questThirteenTip: In a subordinate clause after 'dass', the conjugated verb always stands in the last position.
orderValleyQuestTwo.questFourteen: Select: He is silent because he is tired.
orderValleyQuestTwo.questFourteenTip: The conjunction 'da' (since) — verb at the end.
orderValleyQuestTwo.questFifteen: Assemble: I don't know when he will come.
orderValleyQuestTwo.questFifteenTip: Question words (wann, wo, wie) in the middle of a sentence send the verb to the very end.

orderValleyQuestThree.title: Nebensatz: Level Up
orderValleyQuestThree.description: Consolidating subordinate clauses. The phrases are longer now, but the rule hasn't changed: verb at the very end.
orderValleyQuestThree.goal: Practice word order in more complex sentences with circumstances and modal verbs.
orderValleyQuestThree.questOne: Assemble: I cannot come because I have to work.
orderValleyQuestThree.questOneTip: In a subordinate clause with a modal verb, the modal verb stands last. You can find the list of modal verbs in the Learn section.
orderValleyQuestThree.questTwo: Select: I'm not sure if he's coming to the party today.
orderValleyQuestThree.questTwoTip: In a subordinate clause after the conjunction 'ob', the conjugated verb is at the very end.
orderValleyQuestThree.questThreeTip: Two verbs at the end: main verb, then modal verb.
orderValleyQuestThree.questFour: Assemble: I wonder why he sold his new car.
orderValleyQuestThree.questFourTip: In the past tense (Perfekt), the verb 'hat' moves past the participle to the very end.
orderValleyQuestThree.questFive: Select: Tell me where you bought this coat.
orderValleyQuestThree.questFiveTip: In a subordinate clause (indirect question), the auxiliary verb must be at the very end.
orderValleyQuestThree.questSix: Assemble: He is tired although he slept for a long time.
orderValleyQuestThree.questSixTip: In Perfekt, the auxiliary verb must be at the very end, after the participle.
orderValleyQuestThree.questSevenTip: The conjugated verb stands at the end.
orderValleyQuestThree.questEight: Select: Do you know how much these brown shoes cost?
orderValleyQuestThree.questEightTip: The question word 'wie viel' requires the verb at the end of the phrase.
orderValleyQuestThree.questNine: Assemble: I will call you as soon as I finish eating.
orderValleyQuestThree.questNineTip: After 'sobald' (as soon as), the conjugated verb goes to the end.
orderValleyQuestThree.questTenTip: The modal verb stands in the very last position.
